chapters.title: Before Baseball
chapters.teaser: The American hat economy before the game had a cap at all, from beaver felt to the silk top hat.
chapters.blurb: Before there was a baseball cap there was a hat trade: beaver felt, silk top hats, and a young country already using headwear to signal class and work. This opening chapter sets the stage for everything the cap would later inherit.

chapters.title: The Long Silhouette
chapters.teaser: Eight wool panels on a field in South Brooklyn, and the shape that has barely changed in 150 years.
chapters.blurb: The story starts with the earliest ballplayers and the long-billed wool caps that set a silhouette we still recognize today. A look at how a working piece of athletic gear became a fixed part of the American wardrobe.

chapters.title: The Negro Leagues
chapters.teaser: The caps of the Negro Leagues and the design legacy that mainstream baseball spent decades catching up to.
chapters.blurb: The Negro Leagues produced some of the most striking team identities in baseball history, and their caps carry a design and cultural weight that still resonates in modern reissues. A chapter on style, dignity, and overdue recognition.

chapters.title: The Man Who Drew the Hat
chapters.teaser: The designers behind the logos, and why the marks on a cap are doing more work than you think.
chapters.blurb: Behind every team mark is a designer making choices about color, shape, and meaning. This chapter goes inside the craft of baseball logo and cap design and the small decisions that turn a cap into a signal.

chapters.title: New Era (1920 to 2026)
chapters.teaser: From a Buffalo startup in 1920 to the 59FIFTY on every head, the company that built the modern cap.
chapters.blurb: New Era went from a small Buffalo cap maker in 1920 to the company whose 59FIFTY defines the category. A century-long biography of the brand, its on-field deals, and the manufacturing changes that reshaped the hat.

chapters.title: Mitchell and Ness
chapters.teaser: The throwback specialists who turned baseball nostalgia into a business.
chapters.blurb: Mitchell and Ness built a business on memory, reviving vintage uniforms and caps and making the throwback its own market. A short chapter on nostalgia as a product.

chapters.title: The Forgotten Capmakers
chapters.teaser: The makers who shaped the early cap business and then dropped out of the story.
chapters.blurb: Long before the modern giants, a handful of capmakers defined how the product was made and sold. This chapter brings the forgotten names back into the lineage.

chapters.title: The Wearable Archive
chapters.teaser: Ebbets Field Flannels and the idea that a cap can be a museum you wear.
chapters.blurb: Some caps are less fashion than preservation, keeping defunct teams and lost leagues alive on people's heads. A chapter on the wearable archive and the collectors who treat the cap as history.

chapters.title: Starter
chapters.teaser: How Starter put pro sports on the street and made licensed headwear a status item.
chapters.blurb: Starter helped move team gear off the field and into everyday street style, turning a licensed cap or jacket into a status object. A chapter on the brand that bridged sports and fashion.

chapters.title: Stussy and the First Streetwear Cap
chapters.teaser: The brand that taught people to wear a cap as identity, not allegiance.
chapters.blurb: Stussy was early to the idea that a cap could broadcast taste rather than a team, helping invent the streetwear playbook. A chapter on the move from fandom to fashion.

chapters.title: The Permission Slip
chapters.teaser: The 1996 World Series phone call that put a red Yankees cap on Spike Lee and changed the rules.
chapters.blurb: When a non-standard red Yankees fitted appeared courtside in 1996, it opened the door to the custom-colorway world we live in now. A chapter on the single moment that gave everyone permission.

chapters.title: The Lo Lifes and Polo Snow Beach
chapters.teaser: The Brooklyn crew who claimed Polo as their own and rewrote who a brand belongs to.
chapters.blurb: The Lo Lifes turned Ralph Lauren's Polo into a subcultural uniform, proving that a community can claim a brand the maker never aimed at them. A chapter on ownership, devotion, and the Snow Beach legend.

chapters.title: Snow Beach Sidebar
chapters.teaser: A short detour into one of the most mythologized pieces in the Polo canon.
chapters.blurb: A focused sidebar on the Snow Beach piece and the outsized mythology around it, and what that mythology says about scarcity and meaning.

chapters.title: The Skate Parallel
chapters.teaser: How skate culture handled logos, loyalty, and the cap in its own way.
chapters.blurb: Skateboarding developed its own relationship to brands and headwear, running parallel to the baseball-cap story and occasionally crossing it. A chapter on a different code for the same object.

chapters.title: The Minor League Revolution
chapters.teaser: Wild names, loud logos, and the minor-league teams that out-designed the majors.
chapters.blurb: Minor-league baseball became a laboratory for bold, funny, and beloved identities, and their caps sell far beyond their towns. A chapter on creativity at the bottom of the standings.

chapters.title: The Mall Monopoly (Lids)
chapters.teaser: The chain that bet a store could survive on hats alone, and was right.
chapters.blurb: Lids built a national footprint on the simple idea that a shop could live on headwear, and its collector pivot reshaped the modern drop. A chapter on distribution, scale, and the mall hat wall.

chapters.title: The Bridge
chapters.teaser: The shops and people connecting team retail to the deep collector world.
chapters.blurb: Between mass retail and hardcore collecting sits a network of shops and tastemakers who move caps and ideas between worlds. A chapter on the bridge that holds the culture together.

chapters.title: Patlanta and the Collector Industrial Complex
chapters.teaser: Inside the collector economy, where shop owners know 500 customers by head size.
chapters.blurb: The deep collector scene runs on relationships, scarcity, and obsessive knowledge, with shops like Topperz at the center. A chapter on what it means when a hobby becomes an economy.

chapters.title: The Show and the Maker
chapters.teaser: The two halves of custom caps: the personality out front and the maker behind the stitching.
chapters.blurb: Custom-cap culture pairs the people who put on the show with the people who actually make the thing, and both shape what a cap can say. A chapter on design, manufacturing, and the hook that tells a story.

chapters.title: LA Bleeds Blue
chapters.teaser: The Dodgers cap as a Los Angeles birthright, from the stadium to the street.
chapters.blurb: In Los Angeles the Dodgers cap is less merchandise than identity, woven through neighborhoods, generations, and Latino LA. A chapter on a single cap that means a city.

chapters.title: The Hat as Status Object
chapters.teaser: When the cap climbed the price ladder into quiet luxury and the fashion closet.
chapters.blurb: The baseball cap eventually reached the luxury tier, sitting beside cashmere and designer labels as a sanctioned everyday status signal. A chapter on taste, money, and the cap as a wardrobe staple for grown adults.

chapters.title: The Hip-Hop Fitted Track
chapters.teaser: How hip-hop made the fitted a centerpiece of style and self-presentation.
chapters.blurb: From the boroughs outward, hip-hop put the fitted cap at the center of an aesthetic and gave it new meaning with every era. A chapter on music, image, and the cap as a verse.

chapters.title: The Backwards Cap
chapters.teaser: Rally caps, Griffey, and the small rebellion of turning the brim around.
chapters.blurb: Wearing the cap backwards carries its own history of superstition, youth, and attitude, from rally caps to 90s television. A short chapter on a simple flip with a lot of meaning.

chapters.title: Fear of God and the New Era of Exception
chapters.teaser: The luxury-adjacent brand that bent New Era's rules and made the exception the point.
chapters.blurb: Fear of God helped usher in a phase where high-fashion labels get exceptions and reshape what the cap signals at the top of the market. A chapter on access, exclusivity, and the new rules.

chapters.title: The Last Independent (Goorin Bros)
chapters.teaser: A family hat company that survived more than a century of consolidation.
chapters.blurb: Goorin Bros has made hats since the early 1900s and remains stubbornly independent in a consolidated industry. A chapter on endurance and the craft of the hat shop.

chapters.title: 47 Brand
chapters.teaser: The Fenway souvenir stand that grew into a headwear powerhouse.
chapters.blurb: 47 Brand started with twin brothers selling souvenirs near Fenway and became a major force in licensed headwear. A chapter on family, hustle, and the pre-broken-in cap.

chapters.title: The Side Patch
chapters.teaser: The small patch on the side that turned an ordinary fitted into a grail.
chapters.blurb: A side patch can transform a plain 59FIFTY into a collectible, encoding stories, anniversaries, and access. A chapter on the detail that built a market.

chapters.title: The Yankee Cap
chapters.teaser: The most recognizable cap on earth, and why its meaning never sits still.
chapters.blurb: The interlocking NY is arguably the most worn logo in the world, claimed by fans, rappers, designers, and people who have never seen a game. A biography of a single, endlessly reinterpreted cap.

chapters.title: The Charlotte Hornets and the 1990s Teal Revolution
chapters.teaser: How an expansion team's teal-and-purple identity rewired sports color.
chapters.blurb: The early-90s Hornets turned teal and purple into a phenomenon and proved how powerful a fresh color palette could be. A chapter on a team that was years ahead of the trend.

chapters.title: The 90s Black-Owned Brands
chapters.teaser: The Black-owned labels of the 90s that shaped headwear and got too little credit.
chapters.blurb: A wave of Black-owned brands in the 1990s helped define streetwear and headwear while rarely getting their due. A chapter on influence, business, and the record set straight.

chapters.title: The Cap in Tokyo and London
chapters.teaser: How Tokyo and London adopted the American cap and sent new ideas back.
chapters.blurb: Overseas scenes in Tokyo and London treated the American cap as raw material and reshaped it as graphic and subculture. A chapter on the cap as a global language.

chapters.title: The Cap and Country
chapters.teaser: The free-cap economy of rural America and a farmer with 100,000 caps.
chapters.blurb: Away from drops and grails sits the everyday free-cap economy of seed corn and county fairs, and one Minnesota farmer who gathered more than a hundred thousand. A chapter on the people's archive.

chapters.title: The Cap as Cultural Battleground
chapters.teaser: Dress codes, bans, and the fights over what wearing a cap is allowed to mean.
chapters.blurb: The cap has been the subject of dress codes, bans, and coded readings that say as much about the rule makers as the wearers. A chapter on the politics stitched into a simple object.

chapters.title: Women, the AAGPBL, and Effa Manley
chapters.teaser: The women who played, built, and ran baseball, and the caps they wore doing it.
chapters.blurb: From the AAGPBL to pioneering executive Effa Manley, women have played and shaped baseball far more than the cap story usually admits. A chapter on the record and the people in it.

chapters.title: The MAGA Hat and Political Caps
chapters.teaser: How the cap became a ballot, and the long history of headwear as politics.
chapters.blurb: Caps have long carried political messages, and recent years made that louder than ever. A chapter that treats the political cap as a phenomenon to understand rather than a side to take.

chapters.title: Cinematic Caps
chapters.teaser: The caps that defined characters on screen and lived on long after the credits.
chapters.blurb: From blockbusters to indie classics, certain caps became shorthand for a character and a moment. A chapter on headwear as costume and cultural memory.

chapters.title: Other Sports
chapters.teaser: Beyond baseball: how basketball, football, racing, and golf wear the cap.
chapters.blurb: The cap travels well across sports, picking up new shapes and meanings in basketball, football, racing, and the college bookstore. A chapter on the cap outside the diamond.

chapters.title: Military and Aviation Headwear
chapters.teaser: The military and aviation roots that fed straight into the modern cap.
chapters.blurb: Service and flight produced headwear traditions that shaped civilian caps in shape, symbolism, and ritual. A chapter on the line from the cockpit and the base to the street.

chapters.title: Cap-Adjacent Accessories
chapters.teaser: Squatchees, stickers, and the small extras that complete the cap world.
chapters.blurb: From the little button on top to flat-brim stickers, a constellation of small details surrounds the cap and tells its own story. A short chapter on the accessories that orbit the object.

chapters.title: The Resale Market and the Authentication Economy
chapters.teaser: Inside resale, where vintage caps get priced, faked, and verified.
chapters.blurb: A serious secondary market now prices, authenticates, and trades vintage and rare caps like any other asset. A chapter on value, fakes, and the people who police the line.

chapters.title: The Hype
chapters.teaser: Engineered scarcity, drops, and the machinery that makes a cap hard to get.
chapters.blurb: Scarcity by design drives the drop economy, where limited runs and timed releases turn a cap into an event. A chapter on how the hype actually gets built.

chapters.title: What Comes Next
chapters.teaser: Where the cap is heading, from new silhouettes to the next wave of makers.
chapters.blurb: The cap keeps mutating, and the closing forward-looking chapter reads the signals for what the next decade of headwear might hold. A chapter on the future of the object.

chapters.title: City Connect, 2021 to 2026
chapters.teaser: The City Connect era and how MLB turned local identity into a cap program.
chapters.blurb: MLB's City Connect program made local identity into a recurring design exercise and a steady stream of new caps. A chapter on the most recent chapter of the story still being written.
